---
title: "Lecture 2: History of Web & Internet"
subtitle: "{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}"
author: "{{< meta instructor.name >}}"
date: "January 27, 2026"
code-annotations: below
format: 
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    code-link: true
    highlight-style: a11y
    chalkboard: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: default
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    theme: 
      - ../course-slides-styles.scss
---

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="custom-title">
[Lecture 2]{.baby-blue-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle">
[History of Web & Internet]{.white-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Week 1 | Unit I: Web Page Designing<br>
{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}<br>
{{< meta instructor.name >}}, {{< meta instructor.title >}}
</div>

---

## {data-menu-title="Learning Objectives"}

[Learning Objectives]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Understand the evolution of the internet and web
- Learn about key internet protocols and technologies
- Explore how the web works behind the scenes
:::

::: {.notes}
Start with the importance of understanding the history to appreciate the current state of web technology.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="ARPANET"}

[History of the Internet]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
**1969**: ARPANET - First packet-switching network
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1983**: TCP/IP protocol standardized
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1989**: Tim Berners-Lee proposes World Wide Web
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1991**: First web page goes live
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1993**: Mosaic browser released - made web accessible
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Explain that ARPANET was funded by DARPA and was designed to survive a nuclear attack by not having a central point of failure.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Web Evolution"}

[Evolution of the Web]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Web 1.0** (1991-2004)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Static HTML pages
- Read-only content
- Limited user interaction
- Server-side focused
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Web 2.0** (2004-present)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Dynamic content
- User-generated content
- Social media & collaboration
- Rich interactive experiences
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Web 1.0 was all about publishing information. Web 2.0 introduced social features and the ability for users to interact and share.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Internet Protocols"}

[Key Internet Protocols]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
[**HTTP/HTTPS**]{.teal-text}: HyperText Transfer Protocol (secure)
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**TCP/IP**]{.teal-text}: Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**DNS**]{.teal-text}: Domain Name System (translates domain names to IP addresses)
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**FTP**]{.teal-text}: File Transfer Protocol
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**SMTP**]{.teal-text}: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="HTTP Request-Response"}

[How HTTP Works]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**HTTP Request**]{.teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Method: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
- URL of resource
- Headers with metadata
- Optional body with data
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**HTTP Response**]{.teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Status code: 200, 404, 500, etc.
- Headers with info
- Body with content
- Cache directives
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .body-text-s .center-text .topbr}
Both encrypted with HTTPS for security
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="How Web Works"}

[How the Web Works]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
1. User types URL in browser
2. DNS resolves domain name to IP address
3. Browser sends HTTP request to server
4. Server processes request and database queries
5. Server sends HTTP response (HTML, CSS, JS)
6. Browser renders the page on-screen
7. Browser loads additional resources (images, scripts)
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the fundamental workflow. Every interaction on the web follows this basic pattern.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Client-Server Model"}

[Client-Server Architecture]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Client** (Browser)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Sends HTTP requests
- Renders HTML/CSS
- Executes JavaScript
- Stores cookies & cache
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Server**]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Listens for requests
- Processes business logic
- Queries databases
- Sends responses
:::
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="DNS Resolution"}

[Domain Name System (DNS)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```
User enters: www.example.com
        ↓
Browser queries DNS resolver
        ↓
Resolver asks Root nameserver
        ↓
Root responds with TLD nameserver
        ↓
Resolver queries TLD nameserver
        ↓
TLD responds with Authoritative nameserver
        ↓
Resolver gets IP address
        ↓
Browser connects to 93.184.216.34
```

::: {.fragment .body-text-m .center-text .topbr}
[This usually takes milliseconds!]{.magenta-text}
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Web Technologies Timeline"}

[Web Technologies Timeline]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
**1991**: HTML - Created by Tim Berners-Lee
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1996**: CSS - Added styling capabilities
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1995**: JavaScript - Made web interactive
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1999**: PHP 3 - Server-side scripting
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2006**: jQuery - Simplified JavaScript
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2010**: AngularJS - Web applications framework
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2013**: React - Component-based UI
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2014**: Vue.js - Progressive framework
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Key Milestones"}

[Internet Growth Milestones]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2000s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- E-commerce boom
- Cloud computing
- Mobile web
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2010s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Smartphones dominant
- Real-time apps
- Machine learning
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2020s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- AI integration
- Web3/Blockchain
- Edge computing
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Open Standards"}

[Web is Built on Open Standards]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
[**W3C** (World Wide Web Consortium)]{.teal-text}

Standards and guidelines for web technologies
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**IETF** (Internet Engineering Task Force)]{.teal-text}

Internet protocols like HTTP, TCP/IP
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**WHATWG** (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)]{.teal-text}

Living standards for HTML
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .body-text-m .center-text .topbr}
[✓ This openness enabled global web growth]{.magenta-text}
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Resources"}

[Resources]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- [Internet Society - History](https://www.internetsociety.org/internet/history-internet/)
- [MDN: How the Web Works](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/Getting_started_with_the_web/How_the_Web_works)
- [W3C History of the Web](https://www.w3.org/standards/history/)
- [Course content: Week 1](/content/week-01.qmd)
:::

---

## {#end-slide data-menu-title="End Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">
[Questions?]{.white-text}
</div>
<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Next: Lecture 3 - HTML Basics & Structure
</div>
</div>
